items.headline: One Marina Gardens sets new price record at $3,290 psf
items.summary: A unit at One Marina Gardens transacted at $3,290 per square foot, the highest ever for the development and a new benchmark among Marina Bay condos.
items.meaning: New Marina Bay condos are reaching fresh price highs, so buyers in this area should budget carefully and be clear about how much of the price tag is purely for the address.
items.stat_label: new price record per sq ft
items.body:
A unit at One Marina Gardens has just changed hands at $3,290 per square foot, the highest price ever recorded in the development and the new high-water mark among Marina Bay condos.

Marina Bay has always carried a premium, but this transaction pushes it to a new level. A 1,000 sq ft unit at that rate costs $3.29 million. Buyers in this precinct are paying for views, address, and prestige as much as for the actual space inside the unit.

If you are shopping in this area, be clear-eyed about what you are buying. A significant portion of that price tag is for the postcode. Ask yourself honestly whether that premium holds its value over your intended holding period, or whether you are paying top dollar at a market peak.

items.headline: Serenity Park owners cut asking price to $440M in second collective sale attempt
items.summary: Owners at Serenity Park condo have lowered their collective sale reserve price to $440 million after failing to attract a buyer at their original asking price.
items.meaning: If you own a unit here, a successful sale means a mandatory payout and you will have to move out, so track how developer interest develops over the coming months.
items.body:
Owners at Serenity Park have returned to the collective sale market with a revised reserve price of $440 million, down from their original ask after the first attempt failed to attract a buyer. Cutting the reserve price is typically a sign that developers were not willing to pencil in the numbers at the higher level.

Even at a lower price, a developer still has to work out what can be built on the site, what they can sell those units for, and whether the whole exercise makes financial sense given current land and construction costs. Collective sales are never a sure thing until a cheque is signed.

If you own a unit at Serenity Park, track this closely now rather than waiting. A successful en bloc means a compulsory payout and a compulsory move-out. Finding a replacement home, factoring in timing, and managing the transition takes more planning than most people expect. Start thinking through that scenario early.

items.headline: Chiltern Park in Serangoon delivered the most profitable condo two-bedders over the past 10 years
items.summary: A data-driven review of Chiltern Park finds its two-bedroom units generated the strongest resale profits of any condo in the area over a decade, driven by location and the project's unit mix.
items.meaning: Buyers hunting value outside the city centre can use historical profit data by unit type to shortlist projects with a stronger resale track record rather than guessing.
items.body:
A ten-year look at resale condo data in the Serangoon area finds that Chiltern Park's two-bedroom units delivered the strongest profits of any comparable project over that period. Owners who bought and sold in that unit type consistently came out ahead of two-bedder owners at nearby developments.

Several factors tend to drive outcomes like this: proximity to transport and amenities, the original launch pricing, and whether the unit size stayed in demand as buyer preferences shifted over time. Chiltern Park's two-bedders appear to have hit a combination that the resale market kept rewarding.

For buyers looking at suburban condos, the lesson is to go beyond brochures and developer claims. Pull the actual resale data for the specific unit type you want at the projects you are considering. Past profits do not guarantee future ones, but they tell you something real about what buyers have historically been willing to pay.

items.headline: Private home prices up 0.5%, HDB resale prices down 0.3% in Q2 flash data
items.summary: Official flash estimates for Q2 2026 show private home prices crept up 0.5% while HDB resale flat prices fell 0.3%, marking the second consecutive quarter of decline in the HDB resale market.
items.meaning: The two markets are moving in opposite directions, so your outlook depends entirely on which segment you are buying or selling in rather than treating Singapore property as one market.
items.stat_label: HDB resale price change in Q2
items.body:
The government's Q2 2026 flash estimates show private home prices nudged up 0.5% for the quarter, while HDB resale flat prices fell 0.3%. That HDB dip is now two quarters in a row, which makes it a trend rather than a one-off.

The two figures are heading in opposite directions for a reason. Private home buyers and HDB resale buyers are different groups responding to different pressures, and right now those pressures are pulling the markets apart.

If you are buying or selling in the HDB resale market, do not take comfort from positive headlines about private home prices. They are measuring something different. And if you are in the private market, a softening HDB market is not automatically a warning sign for you either. Track the segment you are actually in.

items.headline: Weaker job outlook is dragging down HDB resale demand, prices and volumes
items.summary: Analysts link softening employment sentiment to further falls in both HDB resale transaction volumes and prices in Q2 2026, suggesting job market confidence is now a key driver of flat demand.
items.meaning: If you are selling an HDB flat, price it realistically from the start because buyers have good reason to negotiate hard while job uncertainty persists.
items.body:
Analysts are now directly connecting softer employment sentiment to the slide in HDB resale activity. Both transaction volumes and prices fell in Q2 2026, and the job market is being identified as a key reason why demand is pulling back.

This makes sense when you think about who buys HDB resale flats. Most are Singaporeans making a major financial decision that relies heavily on stable income, CPF contributions, and confidence about the future. When job security feels shaky, buyers hesitate or negotiate harder rather than committing.

If you are selling an HDB flat in this environment, price it accurately from day one. Buyers know they have leverage right now and they will use it. Starting high and cutting later costs you time and signals to the market that you were unrealistic to begin with. A well-priced flat is still moving. An overpriced one is sitting.

items.headline: Bidadari 3-room flat sells for $945,000, breaking its own record set just 8 months ago
items.summary: A 3-room HDB flat in Bidadari changed hands at $945,000, shattering the previous record that had only stood since last year and showing how strongly demand is holding in that estate.
items.meaning: Bidadari commands a steep premium even for a small flat, so budget well above what a comparable unit elsewhere would cost and do not expect prices to ease soon.
items.body:
A 3-room HDB flat in Bidadari has sold for $945,000, breaking the previous record for that flat type in the estate. The previous record had only been set eight months ago, which means prices in Bidadari are still climbing even while the broader HDB resale market is softening.

Bidadari is a relatively new and carefully developed estate with strong connectivity and decent amenities, and buyers have been consistently willing to pay a premium for it. A 3-room flat at $945,000 is a number that would have seemed extreme for any 3-room anywhere in Singapore not long ago.

If Bidadari is on your shortlist, budget for what it actually costs now, not what you think it should cost based on the flat size or comparisons to other estates. The premium here is well established and it is not showing signs of fading. Waiting for a correction in this estate while the record keeps climbing is a risky strategy.

items.headline: Future Sembawang EC could still be competitively priced despite new 10-year minimum stay rule
items.summary: Analysis suggests the planned executive condo near Admiralty Walk may still offer attractive launch prices even with the government's extended 10-year minimum occupation period for ECs now in place.
items.meaning: The longer lock-in means you cannot sell or rent out freely for a full decade, so weigh the lower upfront price against that reduced flexibility before committing to an EC.
items.body:
A new executive condo is planned near Admiralty Walk in Sembawang, and early analysis suggests the launch prices could still look attractive relative to private condos even after the government extended the EC minimum occupation period from five years to ten.

The extended MOP is a real constraint and should not be glossed over. Ten years means you cannot sell on the open market or freely rent out the unit for a full decade. A lot can change in that time, including your family situation, your job, and your financial needs. Flexibility that you give up at purchase is not easy to get back.

The case for an EC is still the price gap versus private condos at launch. If you are buying a home you fully intend to live in for the long term and your finances are stable, an EC can still make sense. But if there is any meaningful chance you will need to exit or generate rental income within the next ten years, think carefully before signing on the line.

items.headline: NUS survey finds Singapore's private condo and HDB resale markets are pulling apart
items.summary: Research from NUS finds private home buyers and HDB resale buyers are increasingly responding to different economic signals, causing the two markets to diverge more than in the past.
items.meaning: Do not assume a condo market rally will lift your HDB flat's value too, or that an HDB downturn signals danger for private homes.
items.body:
Research from NUS finds that Singapore's private condo market and HDB resale market are increasingly moving to different rhythms, driven by different buyer groups responding to different economic signals. Where the two markets once tracked each other fairly closely, they are now diverging more than before.

Private home buyers tend to be influenced by investment sentiment, wealth effects, and sometimes foreign demand. HDB resale buyers are far more sensitive to local employment conditions, CPF balances, and what they can realistically borrow and repay. As those underlying conditions pull in different directions, the markets follow.

The practical takeaway is to stop thinking about Singapore property as one market that moves together. If condo prices are rising, that does not mean your HDB flat is going up too. If HDB prices are under pressure, that does not automatically signal danger for private homes. Know which market you are in and focus on the indicators that actually affect your segment.
